title: When the Family Says “Don’t Tell Him”
subtitle: Truth-telling, autonomy, and breaking bad news at the bedside

items.title: The case: a son intercepts you in the corridor
items.content: Mr. R, 68, has just been diagnosed with metastatic pancreatic cancer. Before you enter the room, his adult son stops you: “Please don’t tell my father he has cancer — it will destroy him. In our family we protect our elders from bad news.” The patient is alert, oriented, and has not asked you to withhold anything. You now hold a request that pits a loving family’s wishes against your duty to the patient.
keyPoints: The conflict is between family-requested non-disclosure (“collusion”) and the patient’s right to information.
The default ethical and legal position favours the competent patient, not the family.
Cultural humility matters — but it informs how you ask, not whether the patient may know.

items.question: Why does a competent patient’s autonomy generally override a family’s request to withhold the diagnosis?
items.answer: Respect for autonomy means a competent adult is the rightful holder of information about their own body and decisions. Withholding a diagnosis removes the patient’s ability to give informed consent, set goals of care, settle affairs, and say goodbye. The therapeutic relationship is primarily with the patient; the family’s wishes, however well-intentioned, cannot displace the patient’s own right to know (or to decline knowing). Concealment also risks destroying trust when the truth inevitably emerges.
keyPoints: Autonomy = the patient owns information about themselves.
Non-disclosure blocks informed consent and end-of-life planning.

items.question: How should you respond to the son in the corridor without alienating him?
items.answer: Do not argue or agree on the spot. Acknowledge his love and fear (“I can see how much you want to protect your father”). Explore the worry (“What are you most afraid will happen if he knows?”). Explain your obligation gently (“I won’t force information on him, but I can’t lie if he asks me directly”). Then propose the bridge: ask the patient how much he wants to know. This honours the family’s concern while protecting the patient’s right — and almost always de-escalates the standoff.
keyPoints: Explore the specific fear driving the request.
Offer to ask the patient his information preferences rather than promising silence.

items.title: How the SPIKES protocol structures breaking bad news
items.content: SPIKES is a six-step framework for delivering serious news: S — Setting (privacy, sit down, silence the pager); P — Perception (“What’s your understanding of why we did the scan?”); I — Invitation (“Are you the kind of person who likes all the details, or the big picture?”); K — Knowledge (give a warning shot, then plain-language information in small chunks); E — Emotions (respond with empathic statements, not more data); S — Strategy & Summary (agree the next step). The Invitation step is exactly where a patient may decline information — which is itself an autonomous choice.
keyPoints: SPIKES = Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy.
The “Invitation” step lets the patient set how much detail they want.
Respond to emotion with empathy before giving more facts.

items.title: Why a patient’s waiver differs from a family’s withholding
items.content: There is an ethically crucial difference between a patient who chooses not to be told (a valid, autonomous waiver) and a family who decides the patient should not be told (a third party overriding autonomy). The first is permissible — you document the patient’s wish and identify who they want decisions discussed with. The second is not a sufficient basis for concealment. ‘Therapeutic privilege’ (withholding because disclosure itself would cause serious harm) is a narrow, rarely-justified exception — not a tool to satisfy family preference.
keyPoints: Patient declines to know → acceptable waiver, document it.
Family decides patient shouldn’t know → not a valid override.
Therapeutic privilege is narrow and easily abused — use with great caution.

items.stem: The son asks you not to tell Mr. R his diagnosis. The patient is competent and has not refused information. What is the most appropriate initial response?
options: Agree to the son’s request to preserve family trust
Explain you will ask the patient how much he wishes to know, and explore the son’s fears
Tell the son the law requires you to disclose everything immediately
Disclose the diagnosis to the patient at once, in front of the son
items.explanation: The skilled response neither colludes nor confronts. You explore the son’s underlying fear and offer to ask the patient his own information preferences (the SPIKES ‘Invitation’ step). This respects autonomy while keeping the family engaged. Outright agreement abandons the patient; rigid legalism or abrupt disclosure damages the alliance.

items.stem: When you ask Mr. R how much he likes to know about his health, he says: “Doctor, I trust you and my son — talk to him, I don’t want the details.” What does this represent?
options: An invalid decision because patients must receive their diagnosis
Therapeutic privilege exercised by the physician
A valid autonomous waiver of information that should be documented
Proof that the patient lacks decision-making capacity
items.explanation: A competent patient may choose not to receive information and to nominate someone else to hear it. This is an autonomous waiver, not a capacity problem and not therapeutic privilege. Document the preference and the nominated person, and confirm it remains the patient’s wish over time.

items.stem: Before honouring any disclosure decision, you assess capacity. Which set of abilities defines decision-making capacity?
options: Orientation to time, place, and person only
Understanding, appreciating, reasoning, and communicating a choice
Agreeing with the medical team’s recommendation
A Mini-Mental State Examination score above 24
items.explanation: Capacity is decision-specific and rests on four abilities: understanding the information, appreciating how it applies to oneself, reasoning through options, and communicating a consistent choice. It is not defined by orientation, by agreeing with the team, or by a single cognitive test score.

items.statement: A competent adult patient has the right to decline information about their own diagnosis.
items.explanation: Yes. Autonomy includes the right not to know. A competent patient may waive disclosure and nominate someone to receive information on their behalf; this choice should be explored and documented.

items.statement: Therapeutic privilege permits a physician to withhold a diagnosis whenever the family requests it.
items.explanation: False. Therapeutic privilege is a narrow exception invoked only when disclosure itself would cause serious, identifiable harm — and even then it is contested. A family’s preference is not, on its own, a valid basis for concealment.

items.assertion: Colluding with the family to hide a terminal diagnosis can ultimately harm the patient.
items.reason: Concealment blocks informed end-of-life decisions and destroys trust when the truth emerges.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Hiding the diagnosis prevents the patient from making informed choices about treatment and time remaining, and the discovery of a hidden truth typically shatters the therapeutic relationship — so collusion harms the very person it aims to protect.

items.title: Strengths, limitations, and the assumptions this story makes
items.content: Strength: it gives a concrete, repeatable move — validate the family, then ask the patient how much he wants to know — that protects autonomy without a confrontation. Limitation: it uses a relatively clean scenario and does not fully cover fluctuating capacity, cultural and legal variation, minors or incapacitated patients, or patients who change their minds. Assumptions: the patient is a competent adult, in a single ethical-legal jurisdiction, with enough time, privacy, and a shared language to hold the conversation.
keyPoints: Strength: a concrete, autonomy-preserving script that avoids a family showdown.
Limitation: assumes a clean case; less guidance for incapacity, minors, or shifting wishes.
Assumptions: a competent adult, one jurisdiction, and time/privacy to talk.

items.stem: Why does a competent patient’s autonomy generally override a family’s request to withhold the diagnosis?
options: A) Respect for autonomy ensures patients have the right to their own information, which is essential for informed consent and trust.
B) Family members are legally responsible for making medical decisions on behalf of the patient.
C) Withholding information prevents emotional distress for the patient’s loved ones.
D) Medical confidentiality laws require physicians to prioritize family wishes over patient autonomy.
items.explanation: Respect for autonomy ensures patients have the right to their own information, which is essential for informed consent and trust. Withholding information can undermine these principles.

items.stem: A patient's son is upset in the corridor about disclosing his father's diagnosis. Which approach best balances family concerns with patient autonomy?
options: A) Directly refuse the son's request and insist on patient confidentiality
B) Agree to the son's request to protect the patient's feelings
C) Acknowledge the son's concern, explore his fears, explain your obligation, and ask the patient about his information preferences
D) Dismiss the son's emotions and proceed with the disclosure
items.explanation: The correct approach involves empathetic communication (acknowledging emotion), exploring specific fears, explaining professional obligations, and involving the patient in decision-making to respect autonomy while addressing family concerns.
